original_abstract: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are emerging as highly promising materials for low-temperature heat transformation applications, including thermally driven adsorption chillers and heat pumps. Due to their exceptional microporosity and chemically tunable surfaces, MOFs can significantly outperform traditional adsorbents like silica gels and zeolites in terms of water uptake capacity and specialized adsorption behavior. This flexibility allows for the precise design of hydrophobic or hydrophilic frameworks tailored to specific operational conditions. By utilizing solar energy or industrial waste heat, MOF-based systems offer a sustainable alternative to conventional electricity-dependent heating and cooling technologies. This review examines the current state of MOF development for heat transformation, comparing their performance and hydrothermal stability with classical materials, while also discussing the critical role of shaping and coating techniques in optimizing heat and mass transfer.
humanized_abstract: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are believed to be suitable candidate materials for low-temperature heat transformation applications, especially in thermally driven adsorption chillers and heat pumps. Comparing with conventional adsorbents like silica gels and zeolites, MOFs have outstanding microporosity and surface properties which can be controlled by changing different components in MOFs. Moreover, superior water uptake capacity and selectivity towards specific components can be attained by designing the frameworks to be highly hydrophobic or hydrophilic according to the desired working condition. Therefore, low-grade solar energy as well as industrial waste heat could be efficiently utilized by using the MOF-based system instead of consuming plenty of electrical energy in traditional systems. In this review, an overview of MOF materials in heat transformation application is provided. Compared performances of MOFs with conventional materials and their corresponding stabilities after hydrothermal treatments are summarized. Effects of geometrical structures and coatings or treatments on the thermal conductivity and mass transport of MOFs are discussed as well.
